name: What safety and code issues should I think about when changing or adding a door?
headline: What safety and code issues should I think about when changing or adding a door?

mainEntity.name: What safety and code issues should I think about when changing or adding a door?
mainEntity.text: What safety and code issues should I think about when changing or adding a door?

acceptedAnswer.text: Installing a new door isn’t just about making it fit; it also has to meet building code and safety standards. Exterior doors should provide safe egress, which means they need a clear opening size, proper swing, and hardware that can be opened quickly in an emergency. Locks must be secure but not so complex that they slow escape. In many areas, front doors and certain side doors have minimum width requirements to qualify as exits. Glass in doors or sidelights near the handle usually needs to be safety glass, like tempered or laminated, so it breaks in a safer way if impacted. Energy codes can also apply. In cooler coastal climates, codes often require insulated doors with certain performance ratings to reduce heat loss. Weatherstripping, thresholds, and sealing are part of this performance. Pitfalls to avoid include installing very narrow doors where code expects a full-size exit, using non-safety glass near locks, or changing a door’s swing in a way that makes paths harder to use. For example, a door that now swings into a narrow hallway or across steps can become unsafe. Before making major changes, especially to main entrances, it’s wise to check code basics with your local building department or a professional. That way, you get a door that looks good and also keeps your household safe and compliant.
